source_textbook.name: Stewart Calculus (8th ed.)
source_textbook.section: 3.1 Derivatives of Polynomials and Exponential Functions

extracted_concepts.name: Power Rule
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: If f(x) = x^n, then f'(x) = nx^(n-1) for any real number n

extracted_concepts.name: number e
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: The unique number for which lim_{h→0} (e^h - 1)/h = 1, making e^x its own derivative

extracted_concepts.name: rate of change
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: The derivative represents the instantaneous rate of change of a function

extracted_concepts.name: tangent line approximation
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Using the derivative to estimate function values near a known point

extracted_concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
extracted_concepts.definition_snippet: Connects differentiation and integration as inverse operations

extracted_relations.evidence: The Power Rule provides a formula for computing derivatives of polynomial functions
Both are linearity properties of the derivative
e^x is the unique function that equals its own derivative
The derivative represents the instantaneous rate of change
Tangent line approximation uses the derivative to estimate values
Differentiation is the inverse operation of antidifferentiation (integration)
